g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll explore JButton - the fundamental interactive component in Java Swing GUI applications.
JButton is Swing's clickable button component that triggers actions. It replaces AWT's Button with enhanced features.
g.text: A fundamental Swing component that creates clickable buttons in Java GUI applications.
Part of javax.swing package - improved version of AWT's Button class.

g.data-narration: JButton enables event-driven programming, making apps responsive to user clicks. It supports icons, HTML, and customizable look-and-feel.
g.text: Enables event-driven programming for responsive applications
Supports icons, HTML formatting, and pluggable look-and-feel

g.data-narration: JButton follows an inheritance chain: Component → Container → JComponent → AbstractButton → JButton. Each level adds functionality.
JButton offers four constructors: empty, text-only, icon-only, and combined text-with-icon for flexible button creation.
Key JButton methods include setText/getText for text, setIcon for images, setMnemonic for shortcuts, and setToolTipText for hover help.
JButton uses ActionListener interface with actionPerformed method to handle click events. This is the core of button interactivity.
JButton follows Model-View-Controller pattern: ButtonModel tracks state, UI delegate handles rendering, ActionListener controls events.
Here's a complete example: create a JButton, add an ActionListener to handle clicks, and display a message dialog on button press.
JButton can display icons and tooltips. Use setMnemonic for keyboard shortcuts like Alt+D to activate the button.
g.text: Example 2: Button with Icon and Tooltip

g.data-narration: Multiple buttons can share one ActionListener. Use e.getActionCommand() to identify which button was clicked.
g.text: Example 3: Multiple Buttons with Shared Listener

g.data-narration: JButton has three visual states: Normal, Rollover (mouse over), and Pressed. Customize each with setRolloverIcon and setPressedIcon.
Key difference: JButton (Swing) supports icons and HTML text, while AWT Button is text-only with system-dependent look.
Avoid these mistakes: forgetting to add button to container, blocking EDT with long operations, not checking button source.
In summary: JButton is Swing's clickable button with icons support, event-driven via ActionListener, following MVC architecture for clean design.
g.text: Key Takeaway: JButton enables interactive Swing GUIs with icons, tooltips, mnemonics, and event-driven programming!
